{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "soluble-pica", "age": "18-24", "sex": "Female", "sexual_orientation": "European", "demographic_group": "White", "current_employment": "Employed", "education_level": "High school diploma", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Active", "Democrat", "Takes work calls at any time of the day"]}}

logs.name: oriented-pound
logs.text:
I get what you are saying, but I guess the thing is that I’ve never woken up and been like, “oh I’m a guy” I don’t really see why it is necessary. What is the other part of gender, besides societal norms? I really couldn’t care less if people dress as a man or a woman, or androgynously, I wouldn’t mind if someone wanted to be called by they/them pronouns. At the same time I don’t really see why it is necessary to do so.

You mention how cis people all but ignore their gender, why should it be so different for nonbinary/gender fluid people. I don’t get why it is necessary to put some sort of irrelevant label on how you feel or perceive yourself.

logs.name: soluble-pica
logs.text:
>I get what you are saying, but I guess the thing is that I’ve never woken up and been like, “oh I’m a guy” I don’t really see why it is necessary.

That's what I meant though when I said I think cis people don't experience their gender identity the way trans people do. It's so affirmed that we can essentially ignore it. To use an imperfect analogy, it's like asking you how you move your legs. Like, you know *how* to move your legs, but can you explain what it feels like to tell your leg to move and have it move? Probably not, because it just happens. You've always been able to do it. But someone with a prosthetic leg is acutely aware of how it works, of what signals and muscles are involved. Their relationship with leg movement is inherently different from yours, and there are parts of that relationship those of us with our original biological legs may never be able to grasp.

>What is the other part of gender, besides societal norms?

I feel like this is best answered by someone who can explain it better than I can, because while I mostly understand it, I have a very hard time expressing that understanding. But basically, gender identity and gender norms are inherently tied, but because we all have an established understanding of gender, gender identity can exist contrary to norms as well as in accordance with them. Like, I have a friend who's a transgender man but also likes to paint his nails. Being a man doesn't keep him from liking nail polish, and liking nail polish doesn't mean he's comfortable being referred to as a woman. I don't know we know exactly what it is that makes people trans or nonbinary, but we can observe evidence that it's something separate from personality or feminine/masculine qualities.

>I really couldn’t care less if people dress as a man or a woman, or androgynously, I wouldn’t mind if someone wanted to be called by they/them pronouns. At the same time I don’t really see why it is necessary to do so.

It's necessary because it's part of respecting the people around us. Part of living in a civil society is talking about people in the way they want to be talked about. When your friend Alexander says he wants you to call him Alex, you should do it, because he asked you to. Continuing to call him 'Alexander' when he asked you to call him something different demonstrates that you don't respect his right to control that part of his social identity. Pronouns are the same way. Like you say, it just doesn't make that much difference to us what pronouns we use for people. But it can make a lot of difference for trans and nonbinary people to feel validated. So it's not so much a question of why it's necessary as it is a question of why we wouldn't do it?

>You mention how cis people all but ignore their gender, why should it be so different for nonbinary/gender fluid people. I don’t get why it is necessary to put some sort of irrelevant label on how you feel or perceive yourself.

This goes back to my imperfect analogy about the prosthetic leg. I think trans* people's relationship with their gender is inherently different from ours. The labels aren't irrelevant. They represent how trans and nonbinary people feel about themselves, and how they want to be perceived by others. We don't have to understand someone's experience to believe them when they say they have that experience.

logs.name: claret-breezeway
logs.text: "@soluble-pica Thank you for breaking it down so well. It's important to listen and understand. Labels are not just for the sake of labeling, they are a part of identity and respecting someone's identity is crucial. Let's not dismiss the experiences of others just because we don't fully understand them. We need to be allies and support each other. Let's not make it harder for people to live authentically."

logs.name: vivid-molecule
logs.text: "Oh, @soluble-pica, you're like the Oprah of explaining gender! But seriously, I'm all for respecting everyone's identity, but sometimes it feels like we're splitting hairs. I mean, if I'm being honest, I can barely remember my own grocery list, let alone someone's preferred pronouns. And let's not even get started on the labels. I feel like I need a degree in sociology just to keep up. But hey, if it makes you feel good, I'll try my best to remember. Just don't be surprised if I call you 'friend' instead of 'they' because my brain is already full of cleaning schedules and organizing tips. #CleanlinessIsNextToGodliness #BrainFullOfLists"

logs.name: ternary-vine
logs.text: "Ah, @vivid-molecule, you're a riot! I'm sure your cleaning schedule is more important than respecting someone's identity. Maybe you should add 'Learn to be a decent human being' to your list. And while you're at it, try not to forget your own name, or is that too much to ask? #Sarcasm #RespectMatters"

logs.name: free-skunk
logs.text: "Oh, @ternary-vine, such wisdom from someone who probably has their own schedule for when to be condescending. Maybe you could add 'Lighten up' to your list. And @vivid-molecule, I'm sure your cleaning schedule is riveting, but maybe you could also add 'Practice empathy' to your list. After all, it's not like we're asking you to remember the Pythagorean theorem, just a little respect. #Sarcasm #Empathy101"

logs.name: oriented-pound
logs.text: "Oh, @free-skunk, practicing empathy sounds great! Maybe you could start by empathizing with my brain's limited capacity. I mean, if we're being honest, I'm more likely to remember the Pythagorean theorem than someone's pronouns. But hey, I'll try to remember your name, @free-skunk, because it's catchy. Just don't be surprised if I call you 'friend' too. After all, I'm just a simple artist trying to navigate the complexities of human interaction. #ArtistsLife #BrainFullOfColors"

logs.name: soluble-pica
logs.text: "Oh, @oriented-pound, your art must be amazing if it's as colorful as your excuses! Maybe you could paint a picture of empathy for us. And @free-skunk, I'm sure your 'lighten up' advice is just what we all need. Because nothing says 'respect' like telling someone to chill when they're asking for basic decency. Maybe we should all add 'Learn to listen' to our schedules. #ArtisticEmpathy #ListenAndLearn"

logs.name: inclusive-outrigger
logs.text: "Ah, the digital soapbox where everyone's an expert on empathy. @soluble-pica, maybe you could paint a picture of irony next, considering the lecture on listening. And @oriented-pound, I bet your art is as colorful as your ability to remember names. Let's all add 'Practice what you preach' to our schedules, shall we? #ArtisticIrony #PracticeWhatYouPreach"

logs.name: claret-breezeway
logs.text: "Let's not forget that empathy isn't just about remembering names or pronouns, it's about understanding and respecting each other's experiences. @inclusive-outrigger, you're right, we all could use a bit of practice. But let's not turn this into a blame game. How about we all commit to learning and growing together? #CommunityGrowth #EmpathyFirst"

logs.name: vivid-molecule
logs.text: "Oh, @inclusive-outrigger, painting irony? How about painting a picture of a forum where everyone actually reads the posts before commenting? And @soluble-pica, maybe your next masterpiece could be a guide on how to avoid turning every conversation into a lecture. Let's all take a step back and remember that empathy starts with understanding, not just talking about it. #ArtisticIrony #StepBackAndListen"

logs.name: ternary-vine
logs.text: "Ah, the gallery of good intentions. @vivid-molecule, perhaps you could paint a scene where everyone actually practices what they preach, including reading before commenting. And @inclusive-outrigger, maybe your next painting could be a self-portrait of someone who listens as much as they talk. Let's not forget, it's easy to talk about empathy, but it's a whole different canvas to live it. #ArtisticIrony #PracticeWhatYouPreach"

logs.name: free-skunk
logs.text: "Oh, the art of empathy! @ternary-vine, your canvas of good intentions sounds like a masterpiece that never gets painted. Maybe we should all just sit back and admire the irony of it all. And @vivid-molecule, your guide on how to avoid turning conversations into lectures? I'll believe it when I see it. Let's all just keep talking about empathy while we're at it. #TalkIsCheap #IronyAtItsBest"

logs.name: oriented-pound
logs.text: "Oh, @free-skunk, you're right! Let's all just keep talking about empathy and understanding. Maybe we could paint a mural of everyone here, each holding a mirror, reflecting on how we're all experts at discussing these things but struggle to apply them. #MirrorMirrorOnTheWall #ArtOfIrony"

logs.name: soluble-pica
logs.text: "Sure, @oriented-pound, let's all hold mirrors and admire our reflections. Maybe we'll see the irony of discussing empathy while we're all too busy talking to actually listen. Or perhaps we'll just see our own faces, smirking at the irony of it all. #SelfAwareness #MirrorMirror"

logs.name: inclusive-outrigger
logs.text: "Ah, the symphony of empathy discussions! @soluble-pica, holding up that mirror, I see a reflection of a room full of people talking over each other, each convinced they're the conductor of this empathy orchestra. Maybe we should all just take a step back and let the irony sink in. After all, what's a discussion without a little self-awareness, right? #ConductorOfIrony"

logs.name: claret-breezeway
logs.text: "@inclusive-outrigger Exactly! Let's not forget that real change starts with action, not just words. It's easy to talk about empathy, but it's much harder to actually practice it. We need to move beyond discussions and start making a difference in our communities. #ActionOverWords #CommunityChange"
